item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the start, the premise and the direction it goes first. But the end maybe doesn't quite land for me. The mind inside a planet, the suffering and descent into madness, the motivation driving his desire to come back to earth, that all works. But the way the interactions between later subjects sent in paint him as becoming more arrogant and developing a god complex, that doesn't quite land for me. Desperation, loneliness, sadness and suffering, those are the emotions that seem to be set up. Growing power, intelligence, sadism and malevolence, that doesn't really seem set up and kinda comes out of nowhere. I suppose it did give an interesting perspective on the character of the mind in the asteroid, but I kinda think it should have stopped after the planetary survey logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was wondering why MTF ω-0 Ará Orún didn’t intervene.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gave me some heavy "I Have No Mouth And I Must Scream" vibes. Like, they basically created AM out of a human consciousness. That's why the extreme cruelty of D-43852 works for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
imagine being trapped in space for years and then baby

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking a lot about this one (it is genuinely fantastic), and… well… it seems like there is a really good idea for containment that the foundation eggheads missed.

Why haven't they recreated the mental/spiritual environment on earth, in a controlled environment, and then used it as a proving grounds to train new volunteers in the form of mental warfare that Tareen appears to be so good at? I feel like Tareen has gotten such a head-start on practicing this novel form of warfare that nobody the foundation sends out to do battle with her could possibly succeed, landing in an environment controlled by her and having to learn everything about navigating the mental environment while under her omniscient and hostile gaze. The obvious solution is to put your volunteers' conciousnesses into a test environment, identical to the one on board the Heimdall probe except locked down in a cement box somewhere instead of actually launched. Send a few loyal mobile task forces in there and have them conduct wargames with each other until they have way more experience than Tareen does, until they actually understand how to defeat her.

Hell, if you don't send them out into space, there's no reason you couldn't pull their consciousnesses back into their bodies afterward.

I dunno, it just seems sort of obvious to me, that Tareen has the first-mover advantage, can completely dominate any incoming conciousnessed before they even become oriented to their new existence, and that her advantage continues to snowball the more people they send out. But I bet a full team of MTF agents, given a few years to experiment and learn about the interface for the environment, if properly delivered to the Heimdall probe all at once, could kick her ass just as swiftly as she's been kicking everyone else's
Love being the 500th upvote on a really good, weird, and experimentally fucked up article such as this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very late in replying, but I just have to point out that both of those were written after this article.

Also, the fact that the Foundation so completely de-humanized her that they never even attempted to return communication to her is a major point of the article. They assumed that she couldn't seize control of the probe and simply didn't bother trying to placate her. They were pretty much just using her as a relay for sensor data.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final 2 logs on the final collapsible really sold the article for me. I don't see a lot of writing that genuinely unsettles me but the idea of using newborn children to prevent the probe from returning is just such a classic SCP way of handling threats. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So if I understand correctly..
Foundation merged few space systems and generated something able to travel faster than light.
Then they merged consciousness of a D-class with the interface(Which was flawed)
Now they have FTL probe controled by…brains. which now need replacement quite often because the original took control of the probe and become able to absorb consciousness of other people coming in.
There are few things here that I would like answers to:
It is mentioned that something hurt him when he does not have new minds to absorb. DOes that mean the people in there are trying to rebel against his imperialistic command?
If even more conscious people would be uploaded to him… would there be a possibility of overthrowing him and creating a hive mind?

Even thought I don't like what is told in there(Because the babies in the end), it definitely ring a tune in me. Which means it is well written +1
Also Does not foundation thought about what could possibly happen if the consciousness inside would hack through the probe to foundation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'We're going faster than light and instantly transmitting, we don't know how we did it, but we did.'

From what I understand the transmission is facilitated by the human mind in it via phychotronic magic bullshit.

The speed meaning that it would still take ~700 years to get out there.

I fail to see how that's a "flaw".
? Why is this an SCP in the first place? The foundation built it, they know what's going on, it's a totally explainable system. Something being a threat doesn't make it mysterious.
The Foundation made it using the aforementioned phychotronic magic bullshit.

There are LOTS of alien species in the SCP universe, did they just not allow D-43852 to ever have that information?

Most of the time when aliens come up in an SCP it's aliens from another dimension or universe, not extra-terrestrials. There's a few on those too but apparently those aren't canon to this.

Is there something specific that requires humans? It's never established.

Again, brain magic stuff. Animal brain wouldn't work.

D-43852 wants back in their body. Why not send proof of destroying it? If that breaks the system some how that's even better.

From what I understand she doesn't want back in her body, she wants back to Earth. Even if she destroys it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one just has too many flaws for me:

  • 'We're going faster than light and instantly transmitting, we don't know how we did it, but we did.'
  • The speed meaning that it would still take ~700 years to get out there.
  • D-43852 piloting the probe straight towards Earth instead of near by and then coming in instead of taking a crash course which concerns the foundation.
  • Why not pilot it into another planet?
  • Why is this an SCP in the first place? The foundation built it, they know what's going on, it's a totally explainable system. Something being a threat doesn't make it mysterious.
  • There are LOTS of alien species in the SCP universe, did they just not allow D-43852 to ever have that information?
  • Why throw babies at it? Is there something specific that requires humans? It's never established.
  • D-43852 wants back in their body. Why not send proof of destroying it? If that breaks the system some how that's even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They didn't pilot it into a star, it just happens to be near a supernova.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The section about its FTL capabilities references the old version of SCP-2722. That should probably be changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If a baby works, why not just use a lesser lifeform if that would also work? pigs? cats? much easier to bombard with a bunch of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First collapsible, final entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if this was asked, but why not use the safe mode controls to fly it into a star?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, even though it's genuinely horrible.
I don't really understand why they don't find a better way of handling the problem though (other than for the purposes of the narrative, and typical Foundation tunnel vision.) It's fairly clear that there's something wrong with the device containing her mind, hence the voices she hears, so if the device is the problem anybody who's uploaded into it will end up the same way. It would be more sensible to either try to destroy the object, or try and upload somebody infected with a cognitohazard or something like that. Seems more economical than feeding it babies forever. I suppose often the point of the article is that we can see things the Foundation can't.

Is the reason they don't just destroy her body because it wouldn't work (she just thinks it would) or because they need her there to be able to access her logs and gather more information?

Either way, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if they just send the probe toward the nearest planet to crash there rather than into Earth?

+1 regardless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe time to send Adnan to this satellite?
Congratulations. This is a captivating story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If she went violently insane because she didn't find any alien civilizations, couldn't the foundation just transmit the locations of the planet of insect-parasitized communists or the Lob-Star to her?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…holy shit this one's intense.

Take my +1 and go, Kalinin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well then, that changes things entirely. You've actually made this skip make sense. Very interesting headcanon, kudos!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm newly a member here - although by no means a new reader - and this article gains the (admittedly unprestigious) honor of being my first upvote.
I have a theory about this article that explains some of the more suspicious details strewn throughout it.
Khevtuul 1 is not, as this article states, a probe. It was never intended to be. Project Heimdall was referenced before, in SCP-1342, in the context of the Foundation utterly wiping out another alien species out of fear of attack. That's what 2669 is for. At some point, O5 command had an idea: we have all these horrifyingly dangerous objects in containment, some of which seem so nasty that people have suggested trying to toss them into deep space as a way to stop having to deal with them. How about, they said, we not only do that, but we put the skip in a faster-than-light space probe, parade it around to all the extrasolar planets where scary-looking civilizations are developing, and use it to destroy them before they destroy us?
I couldn't say for sure which object they picked, but whatever it is, it activates on its own (meaning that, crucially, they didn't need to let Dr. Tareen know it was on board) and it doesn't leave any records, traces, or memories of its use (so when she approached each planet, she never realized that the civilization that should have been there had been hours or minutes before). Maybe it's something like SCP-2470 that selectively deletes things from existence, or something really esoteric like 055. Whatever the case, it's no surprise that Tareen's sudden desire to return home isn't something the O5s are particularly on board with.
It's mentioned that the superluminal communication thing is contingent on Tareen's body back on Earth staying alive. People have asked why they don't just kill her and let the probe drift. The answer to that is that the spacecraft isn't far enough away yet. If they just leave it dead in the water, the SCP on board is going to breach containment, and at its present distance from Earth, we're still in range of whatever havoc it can wreak. O5 command has really painted themselves into a corner with this one - it won't be an easy task to come clean about this to the people responsible for the project who still think this was an exploratory mission, and if they lose control of the ship, then game over, we're dead.

I of course have no idea if any of this was intended by the original author, but this is my headcanon and I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is interesting, but as others have pointed out, "over 5.3 c" implies not MUCH over, which means this journey would take thousands of years. Also, the Foundation would have to be idiots not to run extensive psychological tests on all potential volunteers, which should have screened Dr. Tareen out. Sure, most people would go at least a little crazy out there eventually, but she starts to crack after only a few failures, then goes full psychopath. And they can't come up with any other ideas besides throwing more and more people at her? Come ON.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here is where I have an issue.

+ show block
– hide block

I know we want to secure contain and protect because we don't understand stuff and might be making a fatal mistake but this thing seems too dangerous to not destroy.

I see people noting upload an animal but I don't see anyone suggesting when d is distracted to send this into a star at a controlled speed.

Sure that may cause some really terrible crap in the universe if the speed is off but the last planet check to me seemed to say they did not slow down on approach and destroyed the last planet. That or they checked, nothing, mad with anger and destroyed it. To me that says they are already breaking crap all over the universe without a care (just not logged and at the least they have the ability to do so) because they are insane and don't even care about their loved ones who are alive at this point.

So again risk D causes the terrible universe ending thing when they have a chance or distract them and make a controlled landing on a star and neutralize this. I don't really see why a baby or half the uploads had to be done. The ethics committee would be pissed if they figured this out and the foundation just kept trying to protect these out of control experiment by feeding it unwilling scientists who are now D class all of a sudden not even touching a baby yet who could not make a conscious choice to agree or disagree because they are a freaking baby. I think the ethics committee would know who needs to be uploaded next to 'contain' this and once all of them are done a controlled obliteration via a star would be done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you are right.

I would like to point out this line of questions ng seems unnecessary. Each entry notes they don't see any previous signs of life as in ruins or some such
So even if the D class messed up and destroyed each planets life I would be expecting to see reports of buildings etc.

The last entry may have been them saying screw it and blowing up the planet on purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the story here, I like the way it was presented, and I like how the cross-links were done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this explanation. It obviously traveled thousands of light years while it was still under the Foundation's control. The math here bothers me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it would be really difficult to time it correctly, especially with 2669 actively attempting to avoid it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always seen the ethics committee being about finding the solutions with the fewest deaths.

If the foundation put it out there, couldn't they make another one to smash into it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shooting toddlers at an evil probe from outer space is way better than shooting aged people at the evil probe from outer space. The Ethics Committee would have no problem unless the toddler-shooting was unnecessary and easily replaced with something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. That poor baby, doomed to sit in a space shuttle forever, never dying, playing cat and mouse with an evil consciousness

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I liked it. Then I've realized it's basically "let's shoot toddlers at evil probe from outer space because why not, it may work". And yup, another skip from alternate universe without Ethics Committee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe if there are multiple Khevtuuls out there, they were launched close together, before Khevtuul 1 went rogue. Maybe Dr. Tareen wasn't psychologically prepared for the failure to find life, while others controlling the other probes merely accepted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Even travelling at 5.3 c, it would take 717 years to travel 3,800 light-years, and the article makes it seem that the probe got there a lot faster than that.

2) If they need to keep the body containing the original consciousness alive, why don't they just destroy the body of D-43852?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They really didn't learn their lesson after the first one, did they.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, if the probe is called Khevtuul 1, doesn't the '1' imply there are more of these consciousness controlled craft out there? o.O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hot boogity that was a fun read getting to that end! Well done with this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is the foundation so terrified of the probe colliding with earth? Presumably it can stop safely upon arriving at it's destination

They're worried it can't or won't stop or slow itself.

Fun fact, an object going at 99% the speed of light delivers its weight in antimatter in terms of energy release should it collide with something.

This SCP is going five times faster than the speed of light. Do you want to risk it colliding with Earth at that speed in the hopes that it has the presence of mind to slow down first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is the foundation so terrified of the probe colliding with earth? Presumably it can stop safely upon arriving at it's destination… Unless it was destroying all those exoplanets it surveyed? Or are they worried that the pilot has become so unhinged he may intend to purposefully annihilate the earth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fantastic read. I love it when an SCP unfolds so slowly and ends with something so shocking. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now all I have to do is imagine her talking in GLaDOS's voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure causality might not be a thing, but then they probably ought to be worried that it will come back before they even sent it. I think I would like it better if this was at least lampshaded.

Mainly though this seems like it violates the central tenets of the Heimdall canon.

I think this may be the first article by Kallinin that I didn't absolutely love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's still an open question about whether the universe really "cares" if you violate causality. Local Realism's been punched full of holes at this point, so there's no real reason to assume that there's any hard rule against FTL, retrocausality, and action at a distance. Could be like the double slit experiment, where the universe just ignores the blatant paradox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heimdall stuff is supposed to be hard SF, and SCP-2272 is very specifically an STL craft with a fusion pulse propulsion drive.

This seems to retain general relativity while having both FTL travel and instant or near-instant FTL communication. So doesn't this violate causality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just send it a cognitohazard, it's still human effectively. Then send someone out there who is not insane or stupid.

item.title: Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Maybe upload something other than human? A fly is my thought, though that might be too referential.

I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is… holy shit, this is well done.

We have a writing guideline that warns against writing something that has to be fed D-class just for the evilz.

I have another writing guideline that violation of the guidelines should only be done if it can be pulled off perfectly. A corner case of that particular trope, if you will.

This is how one does that. This is fantastic work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wonder what would happen if someone with split/multiple personalities was introduced. I know he would still absorb them but would it take longer? Would only one be introduced?

Also this is very terrifying, creating a mind that only gets smarter as you try and stop it, what an incredible concept.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept of this article had a lot going for it, and could have been a contender against SCP-2000 in terms of scale. I understand that this was written for the D-Class contest, but I'm not sure what happened. Downvoted, and I'll explain why.

The premise is very interesting, however, reading it is the equivalent of watching Looper and Inception; both movies that establish an extremely stiff set of rules only to casually break them one at a time. The article in terms of exposition succeeds with making the 'rules' of the technology and the D-Class program seem believable, and it seems like it's original theme could already be explored without the extraneous nihilism and cruelty that seems so unnecessarily common for Series 3.

I'm going to be comparing this article to SCP-2003, because I feel like that it succeeds where this article fails. 2003 also has a similar set of rules, but it's success lies in it's ambiguity. The writing is dramatic for it's ambition but not purple enough to seem unbelievable. The rules are respected in process of exploring Deterministic Sets. 2003 was also practical enough to spend Foundation resources on. And, on the other hand, this SCP is supposed to actively perform recon against "hostile extraterrestrials" that have NO proof of existence save for some sort of number that's asspulled for no other reason but to justify the existence of the Khevtuul 'satellite' ('probe' is a lot more of an accurate identifier).

This isn't the first time that the Foundation has experimented with hardware chassis meant for human use. SCP-2003 featured a variant of the BigDog platform as means for the transport of the human neurological system. This article seems to follow suite, aside from the fact that instead of a brain implant, the controller of the probe seems to astrally project for one reason or another. A few problems arise from this:

  1. The prospect of this article's connection to D-Class is loose, and it feels like one D-Class tale and one completely unrelated article fused together with no purpose.
  2. Are we supposed to believe that the Foundation, with access to FTL technology, has no control over resources that could divert the probe from collision? Instead of painstakingly 'uploading' more and more consciousnesses to the probe, why couldn't they have created another projectile instead controlled similarly, piloted by someone who undergoes XX890-V LFO training who won't just go rogue on some fluke?
  3. With the XX890-V LFO system in consideration by the Foundation, why didn't D-43852 undergo similar training? How come the Foundation entrusted a mentally unfit candidate for usage in a probe with the promise of no external stimuli?
  4. Extraterrestrial life is mentioned with percentage figures brought up for the "possibility" that SCP-2669 would just happen to eventually run across a militaristic race. A large theme that could almost surpass SCP-2000 became completely overlooked during the exploration log. Why? D-43852 wasn't discouraged…She just got bored and filled with angst, because apparently this wasn't what she signed up for. The completion rate of her tasks are unbelievably low, why rebel so fast if the probability was already known to be circumstantially low for ET discovery?
  5. Finally, why is uploading more consciousness the only option? With the technology used to literally render people braindead and project their souls to computers, they could have easily enhanced the FTL tech and create a weapon that is capable of intercepting SCP-2669. And, for some reason, with the newly found astrophysics knowledge, D-43852 SURPRISES! the foundation with gravitational slingshot maneuvers that have been known to modern scientists for decades?

And don't even get me started on the baby. That is where I just gave up. The angle of sacrificing babies for the "greater good" has gotten incredibly played out and over-dramatic. Ever since the infamous Montauk, these existential SCPs have become a metaphysical transhumanist's wet dream. Relativity, interstellar travel, unpreventable doomsday, killing babies? This entry really checks off all the boxes.

After settling down from the revelation, I also realized; what makes this about the D-Class? I thought that it was virtue to not toss D-Class at SCPs like they were candy. I guess that's changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It also happens to be some of the most played out on the site which contributes to the complete fragmentation of this site's suspense of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A refugee camp of orphans, nonetheless.

I agree with you completely. At first I thought that it was a fantastic article but I'll admit that what took it off for me was the fact that the original probe controller's madness wasn't because lack of life and total hopelessness, but because it was decided that "oh look im getting insane oh no!!!!" for someone who most probably had understood the consequences of such an expedition. Felt like it sort of draws you under a false pretense of "what if we were really alone" but instead goes into the direction of "i got bored of visiting a couple of planets despite the fact that the possibilities are infinite so im gonna take it out on you"

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't read a mind-flayed baby, I read a newborn that simply hasn't developed any kind of mind yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had actually been looking at my older writing, specifically 555 in light of the contest, and considered editing it just a tad to make it slightly less cruel to the guy; I think you summed up what I think I'd been going for (and why I decided to leave it) with "desperate cruelty."

D-class are a tool, but they're people, and the researchers "using" them are people too.

…who was in charge of monitoring the 555 incident? maybe a tale's in order…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

This one’s intense. Another ‘different’ D-Class, a professional Foundation researcher who was reassigned to D-Class because that’s who deals with SCPs directly, instead of reduced to D-Class. Not a civilian who’d been taken and made into a D-Class.

It’s interesting how he became the SCP, and while the reporting set of logs are a bit predictable they’re interesting. The wonder turning to boredom then anger.

But then, goddamn it, he goes off the deep end and turns into AM. What an unpleasant direction.

That being said it IS an excellent demonstration of the “Foundation sends D-Class at a problem and hopes it all works” trope that really was never super common. It’s unpleasant, but a lot like something like SCP-555 in the Foundation’s desperate cruelty towards D-Class.

Disgusting but fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a line in there that in some cases the probe can exceed 5.3c, so for the purposes of the math we'll just say that there were stretches in which it went faster. I was tempted to revise the speed, but I figured that proviso would cover things for the distances I required.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like what's going on in this one, but one thing bugs me:

If the probe can reach a maximum speed of 5.3c, and the furthest planet surveyed is 3,800 ly away, wouldn't it take circa 700 years to reach that star? Is there something I'm missing here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the article (bolding by me for emphasis):

an experimental drive capable of enabling a small satellite to travel at relative speeds of up to (and in some cases exceeding) approximately 5.3c.

This spacecraft can travel much faster. 5.3c is simply the upper end of Foundation-produced estimates.

And since this thing seems to have achieved singularity/apotheosis and continues to get smarter no matter what the Foundation does, it's conceivable that it's figured out how to get more out of its engines than it was designed for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is terrifying. Good job. Seeing how the doctor turns from an enthusiastic (not sure if I spelled that right) researcher to a sadist is also pretty nice.
